cards.front: What is the primary purpose of an MBES patch test?
cards.back: To resolve residual angular misalignment between the multibeam transducer, motion sensor, and gyrocompass, plus any timing offset between the positioning system and the echosounder.

cards.front: In what order must MBES patch test offsets be solved?
cards.back: Latency first, then Roll, then Pitch, then Heading. Each solved offset must be applied before determining the next one.

cards.front: What terrain is required for resolving MBES roll offset?
cards.back: Flat, featureless seabed with slope less than 0.5 degrees and uniform sediment.

cards.front: What terrain is required for resolving MBES pitch offset?
cards.back: A uniform slope of 5 to 20 degrees, running perpendicular to the line direction.

cards.front: What is the data signature of an MBES roll offset?
cards.back: Two reciprocal lines over flat seabed show data that matches at nadir but diverges symmetrically with increasing beam angle, forming a characteristic 'butterfly' or 'bowtie' pattern.

cards.front: What is the data signature of an MBES latency offset?
cards.back: Two passes over the same feature in the same direction at different speeds show the feature displaced along-track. Displacement is proportional to the speed difference.

cards.front: Formula for MBES position latency?
cards.back: $\text{Latency (seconds)} = \frac{\text{Along-track displacement}}{\text{Speed}_{fast} - \text{Speed}_{slow}}$

cards.front: What line configuration is used to resolve MBES heading (yaw) offset?
cards.back: Two parallel lines run in the same direction, offset so the target feature falls at different across-track positions in each swath.

cards.front: How many lines minimum are needed for a standard single-head MBES patch test?
cards.back: 8 lines minimum: 2 latency + 2 roll + 2 pitch + 2 heading.

cards.front: What typical MBES calibration values indicate a likely mounting problem?
cards.back: Angular offsets > 2.0 degrees for roll, pitch, or heading, or latency > 500 ms indicate a physical problem that should not be calibrated away.

cards.front: What does 'Frown = Fast' mean in MBES operations?
cards.back: When the applied SVP has velocities higher than reality (too fast), outer beams curve downward in a frown pattern. Memory aid: Frown = Fast.

cards.front: What does 'Smile = Slow' mean in MBES operations?
cards.back: When the applied SVP has velocities lower than reality (too slow), outer beams curve upward in a smile pattern. Memory aid: Smile = Slow.

cards.front: What is the IHO S-44 TVU formula?
cards.back: $\text{TVU}_{\max}(d) = \sqrt{a^2 + (b \times d)^2}$ where a is the depth-independent coefficient and b is the depth-dependent coefficient.

cards.front: What confidence level is TVU reported at per IHO S-44?
cards.back: 95% confidence level. $\text{TVU} = 1.96 \times \sigma_d$

cards.front: What confidence level is THU reported at per IHO S-44?
cards.back: 95% confidence level. $\text{THU} = 2.45 \times \sigma_P$

cards.front: How many factors contribute to TVU in the MBES uncertainty budget?
cards.back: 7 factors: MBES contribution, angular motion sensor, vessel/sensor misalignment, sound velocity sensors, heave and induced heave, depth reduction, and underwater vehicle depth.

cards.front: How many factors contribute to THU in the MBES uncertainty budget?
cards.back: 6 factors: positioning system, latency, dimensional control, MBES contribution, heading, and vessel/sensor misalignment.

cards.front: What is squat in the context of MBES draft measurement?
cards.back: The change in vessel trim between at-rest and underway. Typically the stern lowers and the bow rises.

cards.front: What is settlement in the context of MBES draft measurement?
cards.back: The difference in vessel elevation between at-rest and underway. Once the vessel is moving, the hull settles lower in the water.

cards.front: What is the optimal position for mounting an MRU on a vessel?
cards.back: At the vessel's centre of gravity (COG), the point of least vertical movement where rotational accelerations are minimal.

cards.front: What is the ideal horizontal placement for an MBES transducer on a vessel?
cards.back: On the vessel centreline, slightly forward of midships. This minimises pitch and roll effects and keeps the transducer in laminar flow.

cards.front: What is bubble sweep-down in MBES operations?
cards.back: Air entrained by the vessel's bow wave is swept along the hull and passes under the transducer, introducing aeration noise that degrades or blanks the MBES data.

cards.front: What precision is required for time synchronisation across all MBES sensors?
cards.back: 1 ms or better, typically achieved via 1 Pulse Per Second (1 PPS) and a UTC time message from a GNSS system.

cards.front: Can MBES across-track errors from motion data latency be quantified by calibration?
cards.back: No. Motion data latency cannot be quantified by calibration, highlighting the importance of accurate time synchronisation.

cards.front: What does electronic beam steering require in an MBES system?
cards.back: A continuous real-time sound velocity measurement at the transducer face. Without it, beam steering cannot correctly compensate for vessel pitch and roll.

cards.front: What three purposes does SVP/CTD data serve for subsea vehicle MBES?
cards.back: 1) MBES beam steering and refraction correction. 2) Pressure-to-depth conversion for the vehicle depth sensor. 3) Acoustic ranging for USBL/LBL positioning.

cards.front: What is the minimum recommended swath overlap between adjacent MBES lines?
cards.back: 20% overlap for flat seabed in good weather. Rough topography or poor weather may require larger overlap.

cards.front: What is the danger of double-correcting for motion in MBES?
cards.back: If the MBES system compensates for a motion component (e.g., pitch stabilisation) and the same correction is applied again during processing, the correction is doubled, introducing errors.

cards.front: What is the CUBE algorithm used for?
cards.back: Combined Uncertainty and Bathymetry Estimator -- an a posteriori TPU calculation algorithm that estimates uncertainty from gathered bathymetric data.

cards.front: What is the difference between TVG (transverse gradiometer) mode and total field magnetometer mode?
cards.back: TVG measures the magnetic gradient between two closely spaced sensors (for small ferrous target detection). Total field measures absolute magnetic field intensity (for large-scale anomaly detection and background mapping).

cards.front: Why is TVG mode more sensitive to small localised ferrous objects than total field mode?
cards.back: TVG mode cancels out regional field variations and diurnal drift, isolating the local magnetic gradient from small nearby targets.

cards.front: What is the recommended line spacing for operational magnetometer survey work?
cards.back: Approximately 3x the sensor altitude to achieve adequate lateral coverage with overlapping detection envelopes.

cards.front: What is the noise floor acceptance criterion for a magnetometer in TVG mode?
cards.back: Pass: < 1 nT. Marginal: 1-3 nT. Fail: > 3 nT.

cards.front: What is the position agreement criterion for reciprocal magnetometer lines?
cards.back: Pass: target position consistent within 2 m. Marginal: 2-5 m offset. Fail: > 5 m offset.

cards.front: What surrogate target specifications are required for the GMA1000 gradiometer SIT?
cards.back: Minimum 50 kg ferrous material, 0.5 m diameter, steel or iron (high magnetic permeability), with minimum 20 m separation between targets.

cards.front: What does a valid magnetic detection look like in GMA1000 gradiometer data?
cards.back: A dipolar anomaly: a characteristic positive-then-negative (or negative-then-positive) deflection. It must be bipolar, clearly above the noise floor (minimum 3x), and repeatable on reciprocal lines.

cards.front: At what altitudes is the GMA1000 surrogate item test performed?
cards.back: At 1 m, 2 m, 3 m, and 4 m altitude, running two opposing lines at each altitude.

cards.front: What is the minimum SNR for a valid GMA1000 gradiometer detection?
cards.back: Greater than 3:1 (target amplitude vs noise floor).

cards.front: What is the fundamental trade-off in side scan sonar frequency selection?
cards.back: Range versus resolution. Higher frequency gives better resolution but attenuates faster, limiting range. Lower frequency covers more area but with lower resolution.

cards.front: What frequency is typical for SSS pipeline route survey and debris clearance?
cards.back: 300/500 kHz dual-frequency. The lower frequency provides coverage width; the higher frequency provides resolution for target classification.

cards.front: What causes the nadir gap in side scan sonar imagery?
cards.back: The area directly below the transducer where the beam hits the seabed at near-vertical incidence, producing a strong but non-diagnostic return with no useful imagery.

cards.front: Formula for SSS line spacing for 100% coverage?
cards.back: $\text{Line spacing} = 2 \times (\text{Range} - \text{Nadir gap})$

cards.front: Formula for SSS line spacing for 200% coverage?
cards.back: $\text{Line spacing} = \text{Range} - \text{Nadir gap}$

cards.front: Formula for SSS towfish layback (simple estimate)?
cards.back: $\text{Layback} = \sqrt{\text{cable out}^2 - \text{towfish depth}^2}$

cards.front: What is the best method to reduce SSS layback uncertainty?
cards.back: Use a USBL transponder on the towfish, which eliminates the layback calculation entirely.

cards.front: Formula for estimating target height from SSS shadow?
cards.back: $\text{Target Height} = \frac{\text{shadow length} \times \text{altitude}}{\text{slant range to shadow end} - \text{slant range to target}}$

cards.front: What is the difference between SSS detection, classification, and identification?
cards.back: Detection: knowing something is there. Classification: determining what category it falls into (rock vs man-made). Identification: determining exactly what it is (specific type of anchor). SSS can detect and classify; identification usually requires ROV or 900+ kHz sonar.

cards.front: What is the minimum run-in distance for a towed SSS before the start of a survey corridor?
cards.back: 2-3 times the layback, to allow the towfish to stabilise before recording the survey line.

cards.front: What is the SSS verification target selection criteria?
cards.back: Minimum 0.5 m relief above surrounding seabed, isolated (no nearby features causing ambiguity), and clearly identifiable on MBES data.

cards.front: What is the most common cause of SSS position errors?
cards.back: Incorrect heading source. A wrong heading source rotates all contacts around the vehicle position, producing large cross-track errors that increase with range.

cards.front: What SV value should be used when processing SSS data in SonarWiz?
cards.back: The SV at the transducer depth, not the average water column SV. The acoustic pulse travels laterally at transducer depth.

cards.front: What does an SVP (Sound Velocity Profiler) measure?
cards.back: Direct sound velocity via digital time-of-flight measurement over a known acoustic path length.

cards.front: What does a CTD measure, and how is SV derived?
cards.back: CTD measures conductivity, temperature, and pressure (depth). Sound velocity is calculated from these using empirical formulae (Chen-Millero or Del Grosso).

cards.front: What advantage does a CTD have over an SVP?
cards.back: CTD can calculate density (from salinity, temperature, depth, and local gravity) for pressure-to-depth conversion, in addition to sound velocity.

cards.front: What three factors affect sound velocity in seawater?
cards.back: Temperature (~4.6 m/s per 1 degC), salinity (~1.3 m/s per 1 PSU), and pressure/depth (~1.7 m/s per 100 m depth). Temperature is dominant in most survey situations.

cards.front: How does MBES use sound velocity in two distinct ways?
cards.back: 1) Beam steering at the transducer face (using surface SV from hull-mounted SVS). 2) Ray-tracing through the water column (using full-column SVP for refraction correction via Snell's Law).

cards.front: What is the 0.25% threshold for SVP-induced depth errors?
cards.back: A depth bias exceeding 0.25% of water depth indicates the SVP is not representative of actual water column conditions and risks failing IHO S-44 requirements. A fresh SVP cast is needed.

cards.front: What is the calibration validity period for SV probes (SVP)?
cards.back: 2 years (though some clients mandate 1 year regardless of sensor type).

cards.front: What is the calibration validity period for CTD probes?
cards.back: 1 year.

cards.front: How long must an SVP probe soak near the surface before a cast?
cards.back: 10 minutes to acquire ambient water temperature. For an SVP repeatability test, 10-20 minutes.

cards.front: What is the recommended descent rate for SVP/CTD casts?
cards.back: Less than 1 m/s to allow the sensor to respond to temperature and salinity changes.

cards.front: What SVP formula is recommended for depths greater than 1000 m?
cards.back: Del Grosso (1974).

cards.front: What correction is needed for salinity calculations in the Caspian Sea?
cards.back: Add 1.4 ppt to the calculated salinity value before computing density and sound velocity, because the Caspian Sea has a different salt composition than standard seawater.

cards.front: Why should you never extrapolate a constant sound velocity to extend an incomplete SVP cast?
cards.back: Sound velocity changes with pressure (depth). A constant extension ignores this fundamental relationship and produces significant errors in deep water. Instead, extrapolate the last known gradient or use a historical profile.

cards.front: What type of mean should be reported for sound velocity: arithmetic or harmonic?
cards.back: Harmonic mean, not arithmetic mean.

cards.front: What is the difference between a real-time SVS and a full-column SVP?
cards.back: Real-time SVS: measures SV at a single point (hull or DVL) continuously, used for MBES beam steering. Full-column SVP: measures SV through the entire water column per cast, used for ray-tracing, USBL/LBL ranges, and depth computation.

cards.front: What SVP cast frequency is recommended for open ocean conditions?
cards.back: Every 4-6 hours. SV profile changes slowly in deep, well-mixed water.

cards.front: What SVP cast frequency is recommended for estuaries and coastal areas?
cards.back: Every 30 minutes to 1 hour. Tidal mixing, freshwater influx, and temperature gradients cause rapid SV variation.

cards.front: What is the acceptance criterion for down cast vs up cast SVP agreement?
cards.back: Within 0.5 m/s throughout the profile. Larger differences indicate sensor fouling or hysteresis.

cards.front: What is the SVP repeatability test pass criterion?
cards.back: SV agreement between two consecutive casts within 0.5 m/s at equivalent depths. 0.5-1.0 m/s is marginal. > 1.0 m/s is a fail.

cards.front: What is the acceptance criterion for SV/CTD comparison: direct SVP vs CTD-derived SV?
cards.back: Less than or equal to 1.0 m/s at all depths (accounts for formula uncertainty).

cards.front: What is thermal lag (hysteresis) in CTD sensors?
cards.back: The temperature sensor responds faster than the conductivity cell, causing a difference between downcast and upcast profiles, particularly at thermoclines. Downcast is preferred for comparison.

cards.front: What is an XBT and when is it used?
cards.back: Expendable Bathythermograph: measures temperature vs depth using a fall-rate equation. Used in deep water (> 1500 m) where winch-deployed casts are impractical. SV must be calculated using assumed salinity.

cards.front: What are the five types of sub-bottom profiler?
cards.back: Pinger (single frequency, 2-12 kHz), Chirp (swept, 1-12 kHz), Parametric (generated, 2-15 kHz), Boomer (broadband, 0.5-5 kHz), Sparker (electrical discharge, 0.1-2 kHz).

cards.front: What determines reflection strength in sub-bottom profiling?
cards.back: Acoustic impedance contrast (density x velocity) at layer boundaries. Sand-clay = moderate reflection, sediment-rock = strong, sediment-gas = very strong.

cards.front: What does gas in sediment look like in SBP data?
cards.back: A very strong near-surface reflector with complete acoustic blanking (no data) beneath it. No SBP system can penetrate through gas.

cards.front: What does a buried pipeline look like in SBP data?
cards.back: A hyperbolic diffraction pattern below the seabed surface. Burial depth is measured from seabed to the top of the hyperbola apex.

cards.front: Formula for converting SBP two-way travel time to burial depth?
cards.back: $\text{Depth} = \frac{\text{TWTT} \times V_{sed}}{2}$ where $V_{sed}$ is the sediment sound velocity.

cards.front: What are typical sediment sound velocities?
cards.back: Soft clay: 1480-1550 m/s. Sand: 1600-1800 m/s. Gravel: 1800-2200 m/s.

cards.front: What is a 'multiple' artifact in SBP data?
cards.back: A ghost image of the seabed appearing at exactly 2x the seabed depth (and potentially 3x, 4x). Caused by the echo bouncing between the sea surface and seabed.

cards.front: What is the key advantage of parametric SBP systems?
cards.back: Very narrow beam width (3-5 deg vs 15-40 deg for conventional) from non-linear interaction of two high-frequency primary beams, making them excellent for ROV-mounted operations.

cards.front: What is chirp bandwidth for general SBP survey?
cards.back: 2-8 kHz. For deep penetration: 0.5-6 kHz. For high resolution: 4-12 kHz.

cards.front: What three tests make up the cable/pipetracker field verification?
cards.back: 1) Vertical Range to Target (VRT) test. 2) Lateral Offset test. 3) Mobile Pipe/Cable test.

cards.front: What is the purpose of the pipetracker VRT test?
cards.back: To determine how much impact an increase in vertical distance from the ROV to the pipe/cable has on the depth reading generated by the tracker.

cards.front: What is the minimum distance for the Mobile Pipe/Cable test?
cards.back: At least 50 m of cable/pipe surveyed at standard speed and altitude, performed twice at opposite headings.

cards.front: What is background compensation in HydroPACT pipetracker verification?
cards.back: A measurement taken at least 25 m from any target to establish the signal level from the ROV, seawater, and soil. Signals above this level are treated as originating from a valid target.

cards.front: What are the HydroPACT background compensation thresholds?
cards.back: Individual measurements: within +/-10 uV. Post-compensation coil values after departing: within +/-20 uV.

cards.front: What is the Go/No-Go decision matrix for pipetracker VRT?
cards.back: GO: VRT > survey altitude + 1 m margin. CAUTION: VRT approximately equals survey altitude (within 1 m). NO-GO: VRT < survey altitude.

cards.front: What pipe properties increase pipetracker detection range?
cards.back: Larger diameter and thicker wall produce stronger electromagnetic response and increased detection range.

cards.front: What is the difference between HydroPACT 440 and 660?
cards.back: 440: higher frequency, better resolution at short range, suited for surface-laid or shallowly buried pipes. 660: lower frequency, greater penetration, suited for deeper buried pipes and larger standoff distances.

cards.front: How does seabed type affect pipetracker detection?
cards.back: Sand (low conductivity) is favourable with longer detection range. Clay (high conductivity) is unfavourable with reduced detection range due to higher signal attenuation.

cards.front: What is the minimum video resolution for the DVR verification test?
What is the position consistency criterion for video verification reciprocal heading runs?
cards.back: Feature positions must agree within 1 m between reciprocal heading runs.

cards.front: What is the Voyis Observer Pro 3 camera array configuration?
cards.back: Three cameras: centre (nadir, pointing straight down), port (angled 15-30 deg outward), and starboard (angled 15-30 deg outward). Together they provide a wide overlapping swath.

cards.front: What is the required forward overlap for Voyis FDI images?
cards.back: Minimum 60%, recommended 60-80%. Controlled by frame rate, ROV speed, and altitude.

cards.front: What is the maximum acceptable exposure time for Voyis FDI at survey speed?
cards.back: Below 4.5 ms to minimise motion blur. At speeds above 0.3 m/s, consider reducing to 1-2 ms.

cards.front: What is the pre-dive clock sync requirement for Voyis cameras?
cards.back: All cameras must agree with INS time to within 20 ms.

cards.front: What is the post-dive clock drift acceptance for Voyis cameras?
cards.back: Less than 500 ms total drift over the dive duration.

cards.front: What EXIF metadata must be verified in Voyis FDI images?
cards.back: Timestamp, GPS coordinates, camera serial number/identifier, and exposure settings (time, ISO, aperture).
